concept_name: Entropy and Second Law of Thermodynamics
locked_facts: Second law (Kelvin-Planck): no engine can convert heat completely into work in a cyclic process
Second law (Clausius): heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot body
Entropy change: ΔS = Q_rev/T for a reversible process
For irreversible processes: ΔS_universe > 0 (entropy always increases)
Carnot efficiency: η = 1 - T_cold/T_hot (maximum possible efficiency)
Entropy is a state function — its change depends only on initial and final states
truth_statements: The second law of thermodynamics prohibits the complete conversion of heat into work in a cyclic process
Entropy is a measure of the disorder or number of microstates available to a system
The entropy of the universe always increases for spontaneous (irreversible) processes
No heat engine can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same two temperatures
A refrigerator does not violate the second law — it requires work input to transfer heat from cold to hot

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two scenarios: (1) A hot block and cold block in contact — heat flows hot→cold, total entropy increases. (2) Reverse process cold→hot shown as 'forbidden' — it would decrease total entropy. The spontaneous direction is always toward higher total entropy.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Entropy bars for hot body, cold body, and universe — universe entropy increases in allowed direction, would decrease in forbidden direction
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think the second law is just about engines — seeing it as a universal entropy principle that determines what can and can't happen spontaneously is more powerful

simulation_states.label: Heat flow direction
simulation_states.description: Hot body (T_H) and cold body (T_C) in contact. Heat flows from hot to cold spontaneously. The reverse never happens without external work.
simulation_states.emphasis: Heat flows from hot to cold — NEVER the reverse — this IS the second law

simulation_states.label: Entropy defined
simulation_states.description: ΔS = Q/T for reversible process. Hot body loses entropy: -Q/T_H. Cold body gains: +Q/T_C. Since T_H > T_C: gain > loss → ΔS_universe > 0.
simulation_states.emphasis: Cold body gains MORE entropy than hot body loses → total entropy increases

simulation_states.label: Carnot limit
simulation_states.description: A heat engine between T_H and T_C. Maximum efficiency η = 1 - T_C/T_H. No real engine can exceed this. Example: T_H = 600K, T_C = 300K → η_max = 50%.
simulation_states.emphasis: Carnot sets the ceiling — no engine can be more efficient, ever

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts T_H and T_C. Carnot efficiency calculated. Compare real engine efficiency. Entropy change for heat transfer calculated. Refrigerator COP shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Raise T_H or lower T_C — efficiency improves. But η = 100% is impossible.

common_misconceptions.belief: The second law means entropy always increases in any system
common_misconceptions.correction: Entropy of a SYSTEM can decrease (e.g., a refrigerator cools food, reducing its entropy). But the entropy of the UNIVERSE (system + surroundings) always increases for any spontaneous process.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show system entropy decreasing while surroundings entropy increases more — net increase

common_misconceptions.belief: A 100% efficient heat engine is possible with better technology
common_misconceptions.correction: The Carnot limit η = 1 - T_C/T_H is a fundamental law of physics, not a technological limitation. Even a perfect (reversible) engine cannot reach 100% unless T_C = 0 K, which is itself impossible (third law).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show that even at T_C = 1 K, η is still not 100%
